## Figure Type

This is a **composite borehole/well log (геолого-геофизический разрез скважины)** — a Soviet-era standard-format drill hole log combining lithological description, geophysical wire-line curves, and tabular core/sample data. It appears to be a full-depth composite log printed on a large-format sheet, typical of Soviet regional exploration reporting practice from the 1980s.

---

## Coverage and Subject

The log covers a **single borehole** (скважина), the number of which is partially legible in the header but difficult to confirm with certainty — possibly **скв. [illegible]**. The document header (top of sheet) contains Cyrillic text referencing что это is a log **по буренню / по каротажу** ("by drilling / by well logging"), consistent with a standard paired lithological + geophysical log format. The geographic location appears to be in **northern Kazakhstan**, consistent with the report context (map sheets M-42 / N-42). 

The log appears to cover a **substantial depth interval**, likely from surface to several hundred metres (the vertical scale suggests possibly **0–600+ metres** based on the length of the sheet), though exact depth values at top and bottom are [illegible] at this scan resolution.

---

## Key Content

The figure is divided into several vertical columns (tracks), arranged left to right, as follows:

**Left side (lithological and descriptive columns):**
- A **depth scale column** (leftmost), graduated in metres, running top to bottom.
- A **lithological column** showing a graphical representation of rock types encountered at each depth interval, using standard Soviet hatching/pattern symbols (horizontal lines, dots, brick patterns, etc.) representing different rock types (clays, sandstones, limestones, crystalline basement, etc.).
- Narrow columns likely recording **core recovery (выход керна)**, **interval**, and **stratigraphic assignment (стратиграфия/возраст)**.

**Central section (geophysical curves):**
- Two or three **wire-line geophysical curves** are plotted side by side in the central track. These show strongly varying waveforms with large-amplitude excursions, consistent with:
  - **КС (кажущееся сопротивление / apparent resistivity)** — the broader, higher-amplitude curve
  - **ПС (потенциал самополяризации / spontaneous potential)** — a smoother, lower-amplitude curve
  - Possibly a **gamma-ray (ГК)** or **neutron log** as a third curve
- The curves show **multiple zones of high resistivity** (large rightward excursions) interspersed with low-resistivity intervals, suggesting alternating competent/dense formations (possibly carbonates or crystalline rocks) and more conductive intervals (clays, altered zones, or fluid-bearing horizons).
- There appear to be **three or four major geophysical packages** separated by abrupt changes in curve character, which likely correspond to major lithostratigraphic boundaries.
- A **shaded/hatched zone** is visible in the right-central portion of the log at mid-depth, possibly indicating a zone of particular interest (e.g., mineralization, aquifer, or formation marker).

**Right side (tabular data):**
- Multiple narrow columns containing **numerical data** in a grid format — likely recording values such as porosity, fluid parameters, sample numbers, or geochemical assay results at sampled intervals. The text within these columns is largely **[illegible]** at this scan resolution, but the column structure is clearly a standard Soviet petrophysical/analytical data table.

**Bottom section:**
- A separate, slightly enlarged curve panel at the very bottom of the sheet appears to show a **repeat or summary geophysical curve** (possibly a **КВД / pressure recovery curve** or a **temperature log**), plotted at a different (expanded) scale. This is a common Soviet practice for highlighting a specific interval or check section.
